cases.title: Purulent effusion, degenerating neutrophils

annotations.label: Dense aggregate of degenerating neutrophils
annotations.description: A thick sheet of polymorphonuclear leukocytes exhibiting pyknotic and fragmented nuclei (karyorrhexis). This degree of cellular breakdown is typical of a purulent exudate.

cases.interpretation: This synovial fluid cytospin is markedly hypercellular, overwhelmingly dominated by polymorphonuclear leukocytes (neutrophils). The neutrophils are densely packed, with many displaying evidence of degeneration and pyknotic nuclei. A granular, lightly basophilic background is present, likely representing proteinaceous material and cellular debris from neutrophil breakdown. The extreme neutrophilic pleocytosis and presence of degenerative changes are highly characteristic of an acute, purulent inflammatory process, such as septic arthritis or a severe crystal-induced arthropathy.

cases.title: Macrophage-predominant pleocytosis, reactive features

annotations.label: Macrophage
annotations.description: Macrophages in body fluids are typically large cells characterized by abundant, frequently vacuolated or foamy cytoplasm. They generally possess an eccentric, oval to kidney-shaped nucleus with a relatively open, spongy chromatin pattern, helping distinguish them from reactive mesothelial cells.

annotations.label: Macrophage
annotations.description: This image demonstrates a classic macrophage characterized by abundant, highly vacuolated (foamy) cytoplasm. The numerous vacuoles often displace the relatively small, dense nucleus to an eccentric position. This distinct morphology reflects an active phagocytic state commonly seen in body fluid environments.

cases.interpretation: The cytospin preparation shows a moderately cellular pleural fluid characterized by a marked predominance of macrophages. These macrophages frequently form clusters and exhibit abundant, often vacuolated cytoplasm, consistent with a reactive process. Scattered lymphocytes and occasional neutrophils are also present in the background, along with numerous red blood cells. No unequivocal malignant cells are identified in this view. The overall picture is that of a macrophage-predominant reactive effusion, which can be seen in various conditions including resolving inflammation, hemorrhage, or heart failure.

cases.title: Macrophage-dominant BAL with bronchial lining cells

annotations.label: Mucus/Debris
annotations.description: An amorphous collection of basophilic mucinous material and cellular debris. Such background elements are typical in BAL specimens and should not be mistaken for necrotic tumor or organisms.

annotations.label: Bronchial Lining Cells
annotations.description: A cluster of ciliated columnar epithelial cells. These cells, featuring apical cilia and basally located nuclei, are shed from the bronchial mucosa. Their presence is a common finding in bronchoalveolar lavage specimens, representing the sampling of the conducting airways.

annotations.label: Alveolar Macrophage
annotations.description: A large mononuclear cell with abundant foamy or vacuolated cytoplasm and a relatively small, often eccentric nucleus. These cells are the predominant cell type in normal bronchoalveolar lavage fluid.

cases.interpretation: This Bronchoalveolar Lavage (BAL) sample demonstrates a cellular profile dominated by alveolar macrophages, which exhibit characteristic foamy cytoplasm and eccentric nuclei. Occasional clusters of bronchial lining cells (ciliated columnar epithelium) are present, representing sampling of the bronchial mucosa. The background contains moderate amounts of mucinous debris and proteinaceous material, which is typical for a BAL specimen. No malignant cells, fungal elements, or significant acute inflammation are identified in this field.

cases.title: Reactive large cells, mixed inflammatory background

annotations.label: Large multinucleated cell (mesothelial/macrophage)
annotations.description: A notably enlarged cell with multiple nuclei and dense cytoplasm. In pleural fluid, these can represent reactive mesothelial cells or fused macrophages responding to an inflammatory stimulus.

annotations.label: Prominent large cell cluster
annotations.description: A group of prominent cells showing some cohesiveness. While clusters of reactive mesothelial cells can occur, such formations always warrant careful scrutiny to rule out an epithelial malignancy like adenocarcinoma.

annotations.label: Large nucleated cell
annotations.description: A single large cell featuring a high nuclear-to-cytoplasmic ratio and prominent basophilia. Features such as smooth nuclear contours and lack of hyperchromasia help distinguish reactive mesothelium from malignant cells.

cases.interpretation: This pleural fluid cytospin is highly cellular, featuring a mixed inflammatory background rich in small mature lymphocytes, scattered neutrophils, and numerous red blood cells. Of particular note are several very large, prominent cells with abundant, deeply basophilic cytoplasm and large, sometimes multiple, nuclei. These likely represent reactive mesothelial cells or macrophages, though in a clinical setting, correlation with patient history and potentially further studies would be warranted to exclude a neoplastic process. The overall picture suggests a marked inflammatory or reactive effusion.

cases.title: Neutrophil-laden mucus strands, acute inflammation

annotations.label: Mucus strand infiltrated with neutrophils
annotations.description: Thick strands of mucus heavily infiltrated by segmented neutrophils. This finding is a hallmark of acute airway inflammation, often seen in bacterial infections or severe irritation.

annotations.label: Vacuolated alveolar macrophage
annotations.description: A classic alveolar macrophage demonstrating abundant cytoplasmic vacuoles. The vacuolation represents phagocytosed lipid and debris from the alveolar spaces.

annotations.label: Alveolar macrophage
annotations.description: Another representative alveolar macrophage. Note the eccentric, bean-shaped or oval nucleus and the ample, slightly granular cytoplasm.

cases.interpretation: This bronchoalveolar lavage (BAL) specimen demonstrates a background of scattered red blood cells and mixed inflammatory cells. The most striking feature is a large, amorphous collection of pale, basophilic material consistent with mucus, which is densely infiltrated by numerous polymorphonuclear leukocytes (neutrophils). Scattered throughout the field are several large, vacuolated mononuclear cells characteristic of alveolar macrophages. The presence of abundant mucus and a neutrophilic infiltrate is suggestive of an acute inflammatory process, such as an infection or marked airway irritation.
